coredata.dc:title: Anti-corrosion and anti-fouling properties of ball-like GQDs hybrided MOFs functionalized with silane in waterborne epoxy-polydimethylsiloxane coatings: Experimental and theoretical studies
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Ziyang
Seif, Abdolvahab
Pourhashem, Sepideh
Duan, Jizhou
Rashidi, Alimorad
Mirzaee, Majid
Silvestrelli, Pier Luigi
Hou, Baorong
coredata.dc:description: In this research, two strategies including epoxy matrix modification with 3-aminopropyltriethoxy silane (APTES) and polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) as well as loading APTES-modified hybrids of graphene quantum dots and metal-organic framework (F-GQDs@MOF) are used to gain a composite epoxy coating with advance resistance against corrosion and fouling on Q235 steel structures. The X-ray analysis of diffraction and photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and morphology monitoring by transmission electron microscopy indicate that the GQDs are inserted inside the MOF structure through the in situ reaction, and the hybrid is successfully functionalized with the silane alkoxides. The EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF composite coating shows high water contact angle (more than 100°), low surface roughness, high adhesion strength (8.5 MPa which is 2 times more than the EP-PDMS), superior electrochemical resistance (more than 108 Ω.cm2 which is comparable with EP-PDMS sample with 106 Ω.cm2 impedance modulus after immersion in the NaCl solution for 60 days), and negligible corrosion products and blisters after salt spray test. Moreover, the achieved data by the density functional theory (DFT) method show that the functionalization of the GQDs affects remarkably the strengthening of the interface between this moiety and the MOF in such a way to retard the electrochemical reactions. Besides, the results of immersion in Qingdao port reveal that negligible amounts of marine organisms have covered the surface of the EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF sample, while Styela clava and Botrylloides sp. are attached on the EP-PDMS surface. The antibacterial activity of the EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF against Pseudomonas sp. is proved by low attached bacteria on its surface.

row.entry: Stability against corrosive media and improved adhesion strength; low-frequency impedance modulus over 1010 Ω.cm2 after 90 days and 15% improvement in adhesion strength
pH-responsive smart coating with anti-corrosion properties, hydrophobicity and high adhesion strength

Anti-corrosion coating; low-frequency impedance modulus of 3.18 × 108 Ω.cm2 after 40 days of immersion; pull-off adhesion strength of 8.5 MPa

Anti-corrosion properties and high adhesion strength; low-frequency impedance modulus of more than 1010 Ω.cm2 after 42 days of immersion compared to more than 106 Ω.cm2 for neat sample.

Anti-corrosion properties; low-frequency impedance modulus of more than 108 Ω.cm2 after 30 days of immersion compared to 5 × 107 Ω.cm2 for neat sample.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research, two strategies including epoxy matrix modification with 3-aminopropyltriethoxy silane (APTES) and polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) as well as loading APTES-modified hybrids of graphene quantum dots and metal-organic framework (F-GQDs@MOF) are used to gain a composite epoxy coating with advance resistance against corrosion and fouling on Q235 steel structures. The X-ray analysis of diffraction and photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and morphology monitoring by transmission electron microscopy indicate that the GQDs are inserted inside the MOF structure through the in situ reaction, and the hybrid is successfully functionalized with the silane alkoxides. The EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF composite coating shows high water contact angle (more than 100°), low surface roughness, high adhesion strength (8.5 MPa which is 2 times more than the EP-PDMS), superior electrochemical resistance (more than 108 Ω.cm2 which is comparable with EP-PDMS sample with 106 Ω.cm2 impedance modulus after immersion in the NaCl solution for 60 days), and negligible corrosion products and blisters after salt spray test. Moreover, the achieved data by the density functional theory (DFT) method show that the functionalization of the GQDs affects remarkably the strengthening of the interface between this moiety and the MOF in such a way to retard the electrochemical reactions. Besides, the results of immersion in Qingdao port reveal that negligible amounts of marine organisms have covered the surface of the EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF sample, while Styela clava and Botrylloides sp. are attached on the EP-PDMS surface. The antibacterial activity of the EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF against Pseudomonas sp. is proved by low attached bacteria on its surface.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Corrosion and fouling resistant waterborne epoxies

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Inorganic nanomaterials, graphene-based nanosheets, clays, and nitrides/carbides are amongst the most common nanofillers used in polymer matrix in order to optimize the properties of the anti-corrosion coatings [1]. Meanwhile, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), with a structure based on metal ions or metal clusters arranged by organic ligands, show inherent characteristics of three-dimensional framework, high specific surface area, and crystalline micro- or meso‑porous structure [2–5]. Besides, the presence of functional groups in the structure of the MOFs is a spotlight for their facile functionalization [6–8]. In this regard, zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) is one typical MOF, consist of Zn metal atoms and 2-methylimidazole organic linker. The ZIF-8 has outstanding features of the MOFs with special chemical and thermal stability [9,10]. According to a review paper by Jiang et al. [11], the MOFs have versatile applications in corrosion protection systems. Indeed, although the MOFs have been widely used as photocatalysts [8,12], they are regarded as an appropriate candidate for application as corrosion inhibitors [13], corrosion protection nanofillers [14], functional containers [15], and corrosion barrier coatings [16] for retarding the electrochemical reactions on the metallic structures [17]. These properties make the MOFs an appropriate candidate for prolonging the service-life of the coating systems against corrosive media.

In this regard, the MOFs with their organic-inorganic hybrid structure could be used to create high cross-linked nanocomposite polymer coatings with enhanced corrosion resistance [18]. As an example, Chen et al. [19] developed superhydrophobic, self-healing, and corrosion resistant epoxy/perfluorooctyltriethoxysilane (POTS)-modified ZIF-8 nanocomposite coatings. The hydrophobicity of these nanocomposite coatings is due to the rough structure of the ZIF-8 and the low surface energy of the POTS. The excellent corrosion protection is assigned to both barrier effect of the nanofillers in the coating matrix and formation of an air layer with low thickness on the superhydrophobic coatings (which could inhibit the permeation of the corrosion media). Zhou et al. [15] synthesized benzotriazole (BTA) loaded hollow silica nanoparticles with mesoporous structure (HMSN), followed by surface modification with ZIF-8 (i.e. HMSN-BTA@ZIF-8) to use as pH-sensitive nanocontainers in the corrosion resistant epoxy coatings. Their results revealed that the tertiary-amine groups of the ZIF-8 capped on the surface of the HMSN-BTA could act as a chemical bridge between these nanocontainers with epoxy matrix to achieve high cross-linked nanocomposite coatings with low agglomeration. In another research, Ren et al. [20] intercalated green corrosion inhibitor of zinc gluconate (ZnG) into porous ZIF-8 (ZnG@ZIF-8), followed by studying the corrosion protection capability of the epoxy/ZnG@ZIF-8 coating exposed to simulated seawater. The enhanced corrosion protection achieved by epoxy/ZnG@ZIF-8 is related to the barrier role of the ZIF-8 and the corrosion inhibition role of the ZnG through forming complex compounds on the magnesium alloy substrate.

On the other hand, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are consist of less than 10 sheets of graphene with zero-dimensional morphology and lateral dimension < 100 nm [21,22]. The GQDs are highly soluble in water, with large amounts of oxygen-rich moieties (including epoxy, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups) on their edges which act as binding sites with metallic substrates and polymer coating materials [23]. Researchers have shown that the GQDs can be used as an environmentally-friendly corrosion inhibitor [24] or as a nanofiller to improve the corrosion resistance of the polymer coatings [25]. According to a research by Chen et al. [26], quantum dots of graphene oxide (GOQDs) are used as corrosion inhibitors to increase the corrosion resistance of Q235 substrate in hydrochloric acid solution. They proposed that the corrosion inhibition mechanism of the GOQDs is related to the absorption of negatively charged carboxyl groups of the GOQDs on the positively charged Fe2+ ions at the metal substrate/corrosive solution interface; while the negative charged carboxyl groups prevent the adsorption of the chloride ions to the metallic substrate through electrostatic repulsive forces. Moreover, the GOQDs are absorbed chemically through either the vacant d-orbitals of the Fe, π-electrons of the benzene rings, or sharing the lone electron pairs with the Fe atoms. Pourhashem et al. [25] showed that the GQDs are able to improve the corrosion resistance of the polymer coatings. Also, Ramezanzadeh et al. [27] revealed that polyaniline modified-GOQDs could increase the barrier resistance of the epoxy coatings. This barrier improvement is due to the role of the GQDs or GOQDs in filling the pores and decreasing the defects of the polymer matrix, and consequently, retarding the permeation of the corrosive ions.

From the other point of view, biofouling is a detrimental colonization of biological matters on the marine structures submerged in seawater [28], and therefore, developing environmentally friendly coatings to prevent marine fouling has gained a great attention [29]. Accordingly, Arpa Sancet et al. [30] revealed an innovative concept based on the anti-fouling properties of the surface-anchored MOFs in marine environment, in order to achieve reduced release rate of cupper ions into the environment. Shukla et al. [31] modified polyphenylsulfone membranes by adding 0.5 wt.% Zn-based MOF in order to enhance the anti-fouling properties of the polymer membranes. Besides, Seyedpour et al. [32] achieved improved anti-fouling performance through adding GQDs into thin film composite membranes, due to imparting antimicrobial properties to the surface of the membranes in the presence of GQDs.

Therefore, in this research, the corrosion and fouling resistance of both GQDs and ZIF-8 in the polymer coatings are considered. In this regard, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES)-grafted GQDs@ZIF-8 hybrids are added into the epoxy modified with the APTES and hydroxyl‑terminated polydimethylsiloxane (h-PDMS) polymer matrix. Since silane coupling agents with their functional groups can chemically bond to inorganic and organic materials, the APTES is used for modification of both epoxy matrix and hybrids to achieve better dispersibility of the hybrids in the polymer matrix, enhance water resistance, and improve the interfacial adhesion [33,34]. Moreover, the antifouling characteristics of the developed nanocomposite coatings are considered. It is the first announcement about the application of the GQDs@ZIF-8 hybrids for imposing both anti-fouling and anti-corrosion properties to the epoxy-PDMS coatings. Finally, simulations based on DFT method are performed to further investigate the adsorption mechanism and energetics of the MOF on the synthesized GQDs.

The following raw materials are used in this research without further purification: h-PDMS and APTES are supplied by Aladdin Industrial Corporation, China; citric acid, urea, zinc nitrate hexahydrate, 2-methylimidazole (Hmim, 99%), dibutyltindilaurate catalyst, ethanol, acetone, and sodium chloride (NaCl) are supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). The MU-618 as a waterborne epoxy resin with 200–220 g/mol epoxy equivalent and CU-600 as its curing agent with 180–190 mg KOH/g amine value are obtained from Runtan New Materials Technology of Shanghai Co., Ltd., China. The stoichiometric weight ratio for mixing epoxy resin and its curing agent is 2:1.

A 70 mL aqueous solution containing citric acid (2.94 g) and urea (2.52 g) is prepared by 30 min stirring on a magnetic stirrer at room temperature. Then, the solution is poured into a hydrothermal reactor and kept in an oven at 160 °C for 4 h to complete the reaction. Then, the product of the hydrothermal reaction is kept in an oven at 80 °C to dry, followed by grinding in a mortar. The characterization results of the GQDs are reviewed in our previously published paper [35].

A solution of zinc nitrate hexahydrate (1.34 g) in DI water (10 mL) and a solution of 2-methylimidazole (25.86 g) in DI water (90 mL) are prepared in two separate beakers under magnetic stirring to have clear solutions. Then, these two solutions are mixed with each other and stirred at room temperature for 24 h. The product is centrifuged and washed multiple times with DI water and absolute ethanol at 10,000 rpm to achieve white precipitates, and the MOF is derived by drying the white precipitates in an oven at 50 °C under vacuum condition. The MOF synthesis procedure is based on the method published by Chen et al. [36].

The zinc nitrate hexahydrate (1.34 g) solution in DI water (10 mL) and 2-methylimidazole (25.86 g) solution in DI water (90 mL) are prepared in two separate beakers under magnetic stirring to have clear solutions. Also, in the third beaker, the GQDs (0.134 g) are dissolved in DI water (30 mL) through magnetic stirring. The solution containing zinc nitrate hexahydrate is added dropwisely into the GQDs solution, and then, mixed with 2-methylimidazole solution, and the reaction is continued through 24 h magnetic stirring at room temperature. The reaction product is washed multiple times with DI water and absolute ethanol by using a 10,000 rpm centrifuge, and the synthesized GQDs@MOF is obtained by drying the precipitates in an oven at 50 °C under vacuum condition.

In order to achieve the APTES functionalized MOF, GQDs, and GQDs@MOF (regarded as F-MOF, F-GQDs, and F-GQDs@MOF, respectively), 0.5 g synthesized nanoparticles are distributed in the APTES-toluene solution (obtained by dissolving 5 mL APTES in 95 mL toluene) through 10 min magnetic stirring and 10 min bath-ultrasonic treatment. Then, the reaction is continued under reflux condition at 80 °C for 12 h. Finally, the achieved suspension is centrifuged and washed with deionized water, followed by oven-drying at 80 °C.

Herein, the epoxy matrix is modified with the APTES and PDMS, followed by adding 0.1 wt.% silane functionalized nanomaterials in order to develop three different nanocomposite coatings including EP-PDMS/F-MOF, EP-PDMS/F-GQDs, and EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF. The nanocomposite samples are prepared step by step according to the procedure mentioned below: (a) 70 g waterborne epoxy resin is dissolved in 10 mL water. (b) The APTES is dropwisely added into the prepared epoxy resin in step (a) under vigorous stirring for 20 min. (c) The reaction is continued by adding 30 g h-PDMS (25 cst viscosity and 0.95 g/mL density at 25 °C) into the prepared epoxy-APTES in step (b), followed by 60 min stirring and then, 60 min bath ultrasonic treatment. (d) The required amount of dibutyltindilaurate catalyst is mixed with the above mixture through 20 min stirring. (e) The APTES modified nanomaterials (which are uniformly dispersed in DI water by help of 2 h bath ultrasonic treatment) are added into the mixture through vigorous stirring for 30 min at room temperature and probe-sonicated in order to gain a homogeneous dispersion. (f) Curing agent (35 g) is mixed with the above mixture via stirring for 15 min. (g) The prepared mixture is de-gassed and paint brushed on the previously cleaned substrates (Q235 steel), followed by room temperature drying for 10 days and 90 min oven drying at 80 °C. It should be mentioned that the EP-PDMS samples are prepared as mentioned above without adding nanomaterials. The coating thickness is 200 ± 20 μm which is measured by GTS8202 digital gage, Guangzhou Guoou Electronic Technology Co., Ltd., China.

The inherent characteristics of the nanomaterials are studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) through utilizing the related equipments of Ultima IV/Rigaku/Cu Kα, Nicolet iS10/Thermo Fisher Scientific, EscaLab 250Xi/Thermo Fisher Scientific, and JEM-2100F/JEOL, respectively.

The equipments with tradenames of Olympus LEXT 3D OLS5000 and OCA25/Dataphysics are used to evaluate the surface properties of the coatings through laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM) and contact angle meter (WCA) techniques, respectively. The Regulus 8100 coupled with Detector Model 550i are the tradenames of equipments used for scanning electron microscopy (SEM) coupled with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) to consider the fracture surface and the elemental distribution of the coatings, respectively. Besides, the PosiTest AT-M adhesion tester is used for measuring the adhesion strength of the coating to the Q235 steel.

The corrosion resistance of the prepared coatings against corrosion phenomenon in the 3.5 wt.% NaCl solution is quantitatively monitored by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) through using a AMETEK Parstat Potentiostat/Galvanostat (4000+, Princeton Applied Research). The EIS cell is composed of the coated sample as working electrode, platinum counter electrode, and saturated calomel reference electrode. After reaching an open circuit potential with steady state (OCP), the EIS curves are recorded in 105–10−2 Hz frequency range under 20 mV amplitude. The scribed coatings are located in salt spray chamber for 60 days to observe the anti-corrosion performance based on ASTM B117 standard. At the same time, the scribed coatings are immersed in the 3.5 wt.% NaCl solution to observe the corrosion products through SEM/EDS techniques. The antifouling performance of the coated samples is evaluated by visual observation after immersion in Qingdao port, while the antibacterial performance is characterized by observing the LIVE/DEAD Pseudomonas sp. bacteria (which is incubated in Luria-Bertani (LB) media) by a fluorescent microscope (BX-51 Olympus) after staining.

The DFT method is used for the geometry optimizations, energy calculations and investigating the electronic properties of the systems, with the Dmol3 code [37]. We have used the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof PBE functional, including van der Waals corrections with the Grimme method (PBE-D) [38], all combined with the DNP (double numerical with d and p polarization) basis set to simulate the electronic orbitals. To investigate the complex systems, the adsorption energy (Ead) is determined as following:
(1)
E ad
=
E
( GQD − MOF )
−
E GQD
−
E MOF
where the expressions, in the right side of the equation, are total energy for the complex (GQD/MOF), GQD, and MOF, respectively. Thus, the more stable complex shows a more negative adsorption energy. The energy band gap (Eg), i.e. the energetic separation between HOMO (the highest occupied molecular orbital) and LUMO (the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital), is also evaluated. The Frontier orbital approach, based on the Koopmans's theorem [39], is very appropriate in explaining stability and chemical reactivity of molecules in terms of HOMO and LUMO orbitals. Considering these values, quantum descriptors of chemical reactivity including chemical potential (μ) and chemical hardness (η) are calculated by the following equations:
(2)
μ = − 0

)
(3)

)
where, I and A correspond to -E HOMO and -E LUMO, respectively.

According to XRD pattern presented in Fig. 1 (a), the GQDs@MOF shows intense diffraction peaks at 2θ of 7.3, 10.3, 12.7, 14.6, 16.4, 17.8, 22.0, 24.4, 25.6, 26.0, 29.5, 30.3, and 32.3°, assigned to the (011), (002), (112), (002), (013), (222), (114), (114), (233), (134), (044), (244), and (235) planes of the MOF, respectively. The diffraction peaks of the F-GQDs@MOF have appeared at 2θ of 7.3, 10.3, 12.7, 14.6, 16.4, 18.0, 24.4, 26.7, and 29.7°, related to the (011), (002), (112), (002), (013), (222), (114), (134), and (044) planes of the MOF, respectively [40,41]. The intensity of diffraction peaks in the XRD pattern of the F-GQDs@MOF is lower than that of the GQDs@MOF, due to decoration of the GQDs@MOF with silane coupling agent. The FTIR spectrum of the GQDs@MOF in Fig. 1(b) reveals a broad absorption peak in the range of 3175–2675 cm−1 related to the presence of the bonds of –OH and –NH, and a peak at 1848 cm−1 attributed to the bond of C = C. The peaks at 1595, 1427, 1306, 1180, 1148, and 1110 cm−1 are assigned to the C = N/C = C, C = O, CN, CO, CN, and CO bonds, respectively, and the peaks at 994, 948, 911, and 624 cm−1 are due to the presence of the CH bonds in the imidazole of the MOF structure. Besides, the peaks at 755, 678, and 423 cm−1 are related to the Zn-N bonds of the MOF. The FTIR spectrum of the F-GQDs@MOF in Fig. 1(b) has revealed a broad peak with low intensity around 3150–2900 cm−1 (-OH and –NH bonds), with C = N/C = C, CN, C = O, C = O, CO, and CN bonds at 1585, 1457, 1424, 1380, 1180, and 1148 cm−1, respectively. Also, the peaks related to the CH bonds have appeared at 995, 956, and 726 cm−1, the peaks assigned to the Zn-N are observed at 759, 693, and 423 cm−1, and the Si-O bond has appeared at 462 cm−1. For comparison, the XRD and FTIR results of the MOF are shown in Supplementary Information.

The XPS results of the GQDs@MOF and F-GQDS@MOF are presented in Fig. 2 . The survey scan reveals that both samples are composed of Zn, C, and O, while the Si element is detected in the functionalized sample because of silane coupling agent within its structure. Besides, the high resolution XPS spectra are deconvoluted and the detected bonds are mentioned in Table 1 .

The morphologies of the synthesized GQDs@MOF and F-GQDs@MOF are shown in Fig. 3 . According to TEM images, the GQDs@MOF and F-GQDs@MOF represent similar morphology to the MOF; indicating that the GQDs with particle size < 20 nm (as presented in our previously published paper [48]) are completely embedded in the MOFs through a chemical absorption process by creating hydrogen bonds amongst the NH functional moieties of the MOFs and the oxygen rich functional moieties of the GQDs [49,50].

As mentioned before, the synthesis procedure of the MOF@GQDs is based on dissolving zinc ions in DI solution and 2-methylimidazole, followed by dissolving GQDs and magnetic stirring at room temperature. The XRD, FTIR, and XPS results reveal the characteristic features of the GQDs and the MOFs, without appearance of new bonds. Hence, it is concluded that the GQDs and the MOFs are reacted through their oxygen rich functional groups [51,52]. In the case of the silane functionalization, the ethoxy group of the APTES goes under hydrolysis reaction and covalent bonds are created between the silane moiety and the hybrid [53].

The quantitative and qualitative results of pull-off adhesion test on the EP-PDMS, EP-PDMS/F-GQDs, EP-PDMS/F-MOF, and EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF samples are presented in Fig. 6 , and the average measured values for the mentioned samples are 3.7, 7.6, 6.6, and 8.5 MPa, respectively. The results point out that the type of the nanofiller has considerable impact on the adhesion strength, and meanwhile, the F-GQDs are better candidate to increase the adhesion strength compared to the F-MOF. Accordingly, the hybrids are the best adhesion promoters due to meriting the inherent properties of both GQDs and MOF. Furthermore, the images recorded after pull-off test represent that the samples have cohesive or mixed of cohesive/adhesive failure, which is because of modifying the polymer structure with the h-PDMS and APTES. It should be mentioned that a layer of coating remains on the metallic substrate in the cohesive failure, while the coating is completely detached from the metallic substrate in the case of adhesive failure. These silane moieties could generate high cross-link bridging in the epoxy coating and strong Si-O-metal bonds at the coating/Q235 steel substrate interface, leading to enhanced pull-off strength. On the other hand, it is suggested that a layer of silica layer would be developed at the coating/substrate interface, which is vulnerable to micro-cracks and susceptible to cohesive failure [58,59]. The cohesive failure indicates high adhesion strength of the coatings, related to the presence of covalent and Si-O-metal bonds at the interface [59].

The cross-sections of the EP-PDMS, EP-PDMS/F-MOF, EP-PDMS/F-GQDs, and EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF are considered by SEM analysis and the results are presented in Fig. 7 . The samples reveal rough fracture surfaces with high crack propagation resistance. The SEM image of the EP-PDMS (Fig. 7(a)) indicates that the h-PDMS is completely linked with the epoxy resin in the presence of the APTES, and no aggregation or dis-bonding can be recorded in the fracture surface. The nanocomposite coatings have a uniform distribution of the nanofillers within the polymer matrix, and their cross-sections are somehow similar to the EP-PDMS sample; approving that the interfacial interactions between the nanofillers and the EP-PDMS is strong and the silane functionalized nanomaterials are completely embedded within the polymer matrix. This is achieved because of chemical compatibility between the nanofillers and the EP-PDMS, and the hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine, and silane groups on the functionalized nanofillers are active sites to take part in a reaction with the hydroxyl, silane, and amine groups of the EP-PDMS [60]. Meanwhile, the F-GQDs with smaller particle size compared to the F-MOF, are more homogeneously dispersed in the coating matrix and less voids could be detected in the EP-PDMS/F-GQDs fracture surface than the EP-PDMS/F-MOF. Accordingly, the fracture surface of the EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF is comparable with that of the EP-PDMS/F-MOF, indicating the positive effect of the GQDs to bridge between the MOF and the matrix in the form of the hybrids. Fig. 8 represents the distribution of elements on the fracture surface of the EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF sample, revealing that the main constituent elements are distributed homogeneously within the coating matrix.

The Bode and Nyquist diagrams of the EP-PDMS, EP-PDMS/F-MOF, EP-PDMS/F-GQDs, and EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF are shown in Fig. 9 . At first day of immersion, all samples show a high impedance modulus at low frequencies (Zf=0.01 Hz), which is 3.64 × 108, 3.2 × 108, 6.91 × 108, and 6.53 × 109 Ω.cm2, respectively. Among them, the EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF has the highest impedance value implying the excellent barrier performance. During the soaking process, the Zf=0.01 Hz has a decreasing trend, indicating the infiltration of corrosive ions. After 10 days of immersion, the Zf=0.01 Hz value for the PE-PDMS has decreased to 2.12 × 107 Ω.cm2, and the differences between the samples are more apparent as the soaking time increases. The Zf=0.01 Hz values of 1.35 × 106, 3.63 × 107, 2.59 × 107, and 3.42 × 108 Ω.cm2 are recorded for the above mentioned samples after 60 days of immersion, respectively. The Zf=0.01 Hz changes for the coatings during immersion in the 3.5 wt.% NaCl solution are represented in Fig. 10 (a). Besides, the Nyquist plots confirm that the capacitance arcs depend on the time of immersion and the composite type; i.e. the diameter of the capacitance arc decreases by longer times of immersion, and the EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF has the largest diameters during immersion time compared to other samples. Also, the EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF coating has shown the least variations in the capacitance arcs during 60 days of immersion, which is because of its remarkable stability in corrosive media.

ZSimpWin software is used to measure electrical values based on fitting the EIS data with electrical equivalent circuits. The suitable electrical equivalent circuits are composed of solution resistance (Rs), coating capacitance (Qc), pore resistance (Rpore), double layer capacitance (Qdl), charge transfer resistance (Rct), and Warburg impedance element (Zw), as presented in our previously published paper [61]. The derived results about Rpore and Rct are presented in Fig. 10(b) and Fig. 10(c), respectively, and indicate that all samples have high Rpore and Rct values at the first day of immersion, implying the excellent corrosion resistance due to incorporation of h-PDMS and nanomaterials. However, the resistance values related to the EP-PDMS sample have decreased quickly with increasing the immersion period, proving the penetration of corrosive ions and occurrence of serious corrosion phenomenon at the interface of the metal/coating. While, the EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF coating has maintained the highest and the most stable Rpore and Rct values than the other coating systems during the whole immersion time, mentioning the outstanding barrier performance of the EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF coating with the lowest corrosion rate.

The salt spray experiment is the most intuitive way to detect the corrosion resistance of the coatings. As shown in Fig. 11 , the corrosion products are seen on the EP-PDMS and EP-PDMS/F-MOF samples after 10 days of salt spray test, and the failure of these two coatings is apparent after 30 days due to appearance of blisters and corrosion products. Meanwhile, the EP-PDMS/F-GQDs and EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF exhibit excellent corrosion resistance with formation of a small quantity of corrosion products beside the scribes, and there is no corrosion diffusion phenomenon by increasing exposure time. At the 60th day, the EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF has the best corrosion resistance, compatible with the electrochemical data.

Fig. 12 represents the SEM-EDS analysis from the scratch area of the coatings after salt spray test in order to characterize the corrosion phenomenon on the metallic substrates. The results reveal that a high amount of rust is formed inside the scratch area of the EP-PDMS sample, which has high amounts of oxygen (∼33%) and low amounts of iron (∼58.5%). The SEM images from the scratch area reveal that the substrate is densely covered with sphere-like corrosion products. On the other hand, the amount of iron element on the scratch area of the nanocomposite samples has increased significantly, while the amounts of corrosive elements including O, Cl, and Na have decreased. It indicates the barrier effect of the nanocomposite samples and meanwhile, the EP-PDMS/F-MOF and EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF have remarkably lower wt.% of that corrosive elements. The Cl and Na elements are derived from the corrosive media, while the Fe and O are originated from the iron oxides or iron hydroxides from the metallic substrate. Indeed, Fe2O3, Fe3O4, and FeOOH are the main products on the steel substrate [62,63]. Therefore, the results confirm that lower amounts of corrosion spots have appeared on the substrate through developing modified polymer coatings.

Antibacterial property of different nanocomposite coatings using Pseudomonas sp. is shown in Fig. 13 . After soaking for 5 h, a large number of bacteria is attached on the EP-PDMS, proving that the addition of the PDMS to epoxy could not play an obvious antibacterial effect. In the presence of the F-MOF, F-GQDs, and F-GQDs@MOF in the polymer coatings, the amount of the bacteria has significantly reduced on the nanocomposite coating surface; it seems that both the intrinsic properties of the nanomaterials and the nano-roughness of the surface have influenced the antibacterial property.

To deeply observe their antifouling properties, the EP-PDMS and EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF samples are placed in Qingdao port (which has a large number of nutrients for bacterial reproduction), and the changes on the surface fouling are recorded for 90 days. The biofilms and transparent gelatinous substances have appeared on the surface of the EP-PDMS after 15 days of immersion in the real seawater, while the surface of the PE-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF is partially covered by biofilms (as shown in Fig. 14 ). As the soaking time increases, there is an increase in the amount of the microscopic organisms on the surface. Moreover, the EP-PDMS is covered by a large number of organisms (such as Styela clava and Botrylloides sp.) after 60 days of immersion, while the attached area and the amount of organisms on the surface of the EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF samples are remarkably less than the EP-PDMS. Besides, the barnacle larvae, seaweed and other organisms have appeared in the case of the EP-PDMS after immersion for 90 days, and the surface of the EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF samples is mainly covered by barnacle larvae; indicating that the F-GQDs@MOF has antifouling property due to synergetic effects of the silane, GQDs, and MOF.

The obtained experimental data of the present research have confirmed the presence of functional groups over GQDs. Hence, the interaction mechanism of the MOF with the aforementioned moiety, by particular attention to the functional groups, is explored by theoretical DFT calculations. The modulated structures of the GQD, in both of pure and functionalized states, and their corresponding complexes with MOF, are shown in Fig. 15 . The average bond distance of CC in the structure of GQDs is 1.43 Å, which is in good agreement with the literature value [64]. In our calculation, the MOF structure is modulated as shown in Fig. 15(d). Indeed, taking this structure as a reprehensive of ZIF-8 can dramatically reduce the computational cost and, at the same time, provide reliable results [65]. We have also plotted the related graphs of the distribution of frontier orbitals (HOMO and LUMO) in Fig. 16 . As shown in this Figure, these orbitals are more amplified over the functionalized-GQD, compared with the pure case, making this flake more active for charge transfer. This effect could be, in turn, a driving force for an upcoming interaction with other species. According to our results, the adsorption of the MOF over functionalized GQD, with Ead= −349 kJ/mol, is remarkably stronger than that of the pure case (Ead= −188 kJ/mol), showing the positive effects made by functional groups on the stability of the hybrid. The minimum equilibrium distances between these moieties, which are about 2.6 Å, show that the complexes are stabilized mainly through van der Waals interactions. The deformation map of the electron density of the complexes (Fig. 16) illustrates the change of electron density due to the complexation. It is clear that no covalent bond is formed between the surface of the GQDs and the MOF in these complexes, thus elucidating the predominant role of van der Waals forces for the stability of the complexes. Moreover, we calculated some relevant electronic properties of these systems. As reported in Table 2 , the energy gap between the HOMO and LUMO is 0.224 eV and 0.123 eV for the pure and functionalized GQDs, respectively. This value increases for the adsorption of the MOF over pure flake, while it decreases for the adsorption over the functionalized one. Using these quantities, we obtain the corresponding values of chemical potential (μ) and chemical hardness (η) of the systems. Since the functionalized-GQD is characterized by a value of μ more negative and a value of η smaller than that of the pure GQD, this indicates that the adsorption of the MOF on this flake could be done easier. This trend remains the same upon complexation. According to the data reported in Table 2, and the definition of Hard and Soft Acids and Bases (HSAB) theory [66], the interaction of the MOF with the functionalized-GQD should be stronger than that with the pure case. Notice that the HSAB theory states that soft (hard) acids react quickly and develop stronger bonds with soft (hard) bases. We have also performed a charge analysis to better elucidate the process of charge transfer between moieties. Our Mullikan analysis indicates a net charge transfer from the MOF to the GQDs, which is in good agreement with the literature [65]. In particular, 0.812 |e| and 1.235 |e| electron charge transfers in the case of pure and functionalized-flakes, respectively, with clearly a larger value for the later. Indeed, the electron charge transfer represents one important indicator for characterizing intermolecular interactions [67]. In summary, our theoretical investigations underline the key role played by functional groups in the GQD regarding the interaction between this flake and the MOF, thus making functionalized GQD very promising for blocking the diffusion path of corrosive agents and preventing electrochemical corrosion reactions. Also, although the experiments are based on the silane-functionalized samples in this work, our theoretical calculations focus on the interface made by GQD and MOF in the nanohybrid system. It should be noted from the previous researches that this interface, due to more van der Waals forces, can be a very strong even in the presence of silane [68,69].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of corrosion protection and antifouling behavior
ce:section.ce:para: The corrosion phenomenon in a polymer coated substrate is consist of three steps including wetting the surface with the corrosive electrolyte, diffusion of corrosive ions into the coating structure, and chemical reactions at the coating/metal interface. The developed coatings in this research have shown high WCAs of more than 100°, revealing their hydrophobic behavior due to modification of epoxy matrix with the APTES and PDMS. Next, the EIS results show that the developed coatings have high impedance modulus at low frequencies and high Rpore values, approving that the diffusion of the corrosive agents is retarded. The resistant parameters for the nanocomposite samples are higher compared to the EP-PDMS sample, due to the presence of nanofillers in the coating and complexing the permeation path of the corrosive media in the coating [70,71]. Meanwhile, the EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF has shown the highest impedance values, which is due to co-presence of GQDs and MOFs in the polymer matrix. The GQDs with their ball-like morphology and the MOFs with their rhombic dodecahedron morphology could cover each other to fill the porosities of the polymer matrix more effectively in the form of hybrids. Furthermore, the nanocomposite samples have two times more adhesion strength to the Q235 steel than the EP-PDMS sample and these coatings have higher Rct values, indicating that the diffusion of the corrosive media could less affect the coating/metal interface and the corrosion reactions are retarded. Hence, the EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF with high WCA, adhesion strength, and impedance modulus is the most effective sample in providing long-term corrosion protection.

In the case of antifouling properties, the presence of the GQDs and MOFs in the form of hybrid has provided antifouling characteristics to the coatings. The MOFs have the capability of releasing ions to interact with bacteria cell [72,73], while it seems that the embedded GQDs in the coating matrix could provide electrostatic interactions toward the bacteria cell [74]. Accordingly, the adhesion of bacteria on the coating surface has decreased and lower interactions are achieved [75].

Table 3 represents an overview about the research activities directed about the application of MOF-based nanomaterials for achieving anticorrosion coatings, and the results are compared with the present study.

In this research, the waterborne epoxy coatings are modified with adding PDMS and APTES, followed by modifying the polymer matrix with either of F-GQDs, F-MOF, and F-GQDs@MOF. The results clearly demonstrate the importance of nanofillers’ type and inherent characteristics on the performance of the nanocomposite coatings. The corrosion protection resistance of the polymer coatings increases as the order of EP-PDMS << EP-PDMS/F-MOF < EP-PDMS-F-GQDs < EP-PDMS/F-GQDs@MOF, and it seems that the GQDs with their small particle size are able to fill the cavities and pores of the polymer matrix more effectively than the MOFs with rhombic dodecahedron particles (average size of about 60 nm). Besides, the hybrids of the F-GQDs@MOF merit the inherent properties of both GQDs and MOFs, and the results confirm that the hybrid is a better candidate compared to the F-GQDs or F-MOF. Lower amounts of bacteria and marine fouling agents have attached on the surface of the nanocomposite samples related to the effect of the nanofillers on modifying the surface characteristics of the coatings. Finally, the DFT results show that the functionalization of the pure GQDs plays an important role on the corrosion resistance of the hybrid system. Indeed, this could be assigned to the more stable interface formed between the MOFs and the GQDs. It is also shown that the functionalization improves the charge transfer from the MOFs to the GQDs, which could enhance their binding capacity.

Ziyang Zhou: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Abdolvahab Seif: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Sepideh Pourhashem: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Jizhou Duan: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Alimorad Rashidi: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Majid Mirzaee: Writing – review & editing. Pier Luigi Silvestrelli: Writing – review & editing. Baorong Hou: Supervision.
